content.problemStatement:
You are given a tree with NNN nodes (numbered 111 through NNN).

The distance d(p,q)d(p,q)d(p,q) between two nodes ppp and qqq of the tree is the number of edges on the unique path between them.

There will be QQQ events each of which can be of the following three types:



'1 u': A certain node uuu becomes a special node. It is guaranteed that uuu was not special before this event.




'2 u': A certain node uuu becomes no longer special. It is guaranteed that uuu was special before this event.




'3 K': You are given a non-negative integer KKK. For a node xxx, define f(x)=max⁡{d(x,y):y is special}f(x)=\max\{ d(x,y):y\text{ is special}\}f(x)=max{d(x,y):y is special}. You must answer if there exists a node xxx such that f(x)=Kf(x)=Kf(x)=K. In other words, you are asked whether there exists a node xxx whose distance to the farthest special node is KKK. Note that xxx doesn't have to be special. It is guaranteed that there is at least one special node in the tree at the time of this event.



In the beginning, there are no special nodes.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT - the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN.
N−1N−1N−1 lines follow. The iii-th of these lines contains two integers uiu_iui​ and viv_ivi​ denoting an edge of the tree.
The next line contains a single integer QQQ - the number of events.
QQQ lines follow. The iii-th of these lines contains two integers tit_iti​ (the type of the iii-th query) and qiq_iqi​. If ti=1t_i=1ti​=1 or ti=2t_i=2ti​=2, qiq_iqi​ is the vertex whose status is being changed. If ti=3t_i=3ti​=3, qiq_iqi​ is the parameter describing a query of the third type.


Output Format

For each test case, print a single line.
In that line, print lll characters with no spaces between them, where lll is the number of queries of third type in this test case.
For each valid iii, the iii-th character in the line should be 111 if there exists a node satisfying the conditions of the iii-th query of third type in that test case and 000 otherwise.
constraints: 1 ≤ N, Q ≤ 2·10^5 over all test cases
The sum of N over all test cases ≤ 4·10^5
The sum of Q over all test cases ≤ 4·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line N, the number of nodes in the tree. Next N‑1 lines each contain two integers u v describing an undirected edge. Next line contains integer Q, the number of events. Then Q lines follow, each line contains two integers t x. If t = 1, node x becomes special (it was not special before). If t = 2, node x ceases to be special (it was special before). If t = 3, x is a non‑negative integer K and you have to answer whether there exists a node whose farthest special node is at distance exactly K. It is guaranteed that at the moment of a type‑3 query the set of special nodes is non‑empty.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line consisting of characters '1' and '0' without spaces. The i‑th character corresponds to the i‑th query of type 3 in this test case: '1' if such a node exists, otherwise '0'.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 1: The following picture shows the tree in the first test case:




In the first query of the third type we have K=2K=2K=2 and the node 222 is the only special node. The special node with the maximum distance from node 333 is the node 222 and d(3,2)=2=Kd(3,2)=2=Kd(3,2)=2=K, so the node 333 satisfies the requirement. Therefore, the answer to this query is 111.
In the second query of the third type we have K=3K=3K=3 and the node 222 is the only special node. There do not exist any nodes at distance K=3K=3K=3 from the node 222. Therefore, the answer to this query is 000.
In the third query of the third type we have K=3K=3K=3 and the special nodes are 222 and 444. The special node with the maximum distance from node 333 is the node 444 and d(3,4)=3=Kd(3,4)=3=Kd(3,4)=3=K, so the node 333 satisfies the requirement. Therefore, the answer to this query is 111.
In the fourth query of the third type we have K=1K=1K=1 and the node 444 is the only special node. The special node with the maximum distance from node 222 is the node 444 and d(2,4)=1=Kd(2,4)=1=Kd(2,4)=1=K, so the node 222 satisfies the requirement. Therefore, the answer to this query is 111.

Test case 2: The following picture shows the tree in the second test case:





In the first query of the third type we have K=2K=2K=2 and the special nodes are 111, 777 and 888. The special node with the maximum distance from node 222 is the node 777 and d(2,7)=2=Kd(2,7)=2=Kd(2,7)=2=K, so the node 222 satisfies the requirement. Therefore, the answer to this query is 111.


In the second query of the third type we have K=1K=1K=1 and the special nodes are 111, 777 and 888. There do not exist any nodes at distance K=1K=1K=1 from their farthest special node. Therefore, the answer to this query is 000.


In the third query of the third type we have K=4K=4K=4 and the special nodes are 111, 777 and 888. The special node with the maximum distance from node 666 is the node 777 and d(6,7)=4=Kd(6,7)=4=Kd(6,7)=4=K, so the node 666 satisfies the requirement. Therefore, the answer to this query is 111.


In the fourth query of the third type we have K=1K=1K=1 and the special nodes are 777 and 888. The special node with the maximum distance from node 555 is the node 777 and d(5,7)=1=Kd(5,7)=1=Kd(5,7)=1=K, so the node 555 satisfies the requirement. Therefore, the answer to this query is 111.


In the fifth query of the third type we have K=5K=5K=5 and the special nodes are 777 and 888. There do not exist any nodes at distance K=5K=5K=5 from their farthest special node. Therefore, the answer to this query is 000.

solution.approach:
For a set S of special nodes define a = one endpoint of the diameter of S and b = the other endpoint (the pair with maximum distance). For any node x, the farthest special node is either a or b, therefore f(x) = max(dist(x,a), dist(x,b)). Let D = dist(a,b) and let R = max(eccentricity(a), eccentricity(b)), where eccentricity(v) = max_{u in tree} dist(v,u). The values taken by f(x) over all nodes form a contiguous integer interval [ceil(D/2), R]. Consequently a query with value K is answered '1' iff ceil(D/2) ≤ K ≤ R.

The algorithm therefore needs to maintain dynamically:
1. The diameter endpoints a and b of the current special set.
2. The global eccentricities of a and b (which can be obtained from pre‑computed distances to the two endpoints of the overall tree diameter).

Both tasks are supported by a centroid decomposition of the tree. For each centroid we keep a multiset of distances from that centroid to all currently special nodes in its subtree. Insertion/deletion of a special node updates O(log N) multisets. Using the stored multisets we can answer in O(log N) the farthest special node from any given vertex: for a query vertex v we walk up the centroid chain, and for each centroid c we combine dist(v,c) with the maximum distance stored in c's multiset. The farthest node itself is also retrieved (any node achieving the maximum distance is sufficient).

To keep the diameter endpoints up‑to‑date we use the classic two‑phase method after each update:
- Pick an arbitrary special node s (the most recently inserted one, or any element from a global set of specials).
- Find t = farthestSpecial(s) using the centroid structure.
- Find u = farthestSpecial(t).
The pair (t,u) is the new diameter of the special set. Both steps take O(log N).

Pre‑processing: compute the two endpoints p,q of the tree’s overall diameter by two BFS/DFS passes. For every node v store distP[v] = dist(v,p) and distQ[v] = dist(v,q). Then eccentricity(v) = max(distP[v], distQ[v]).

Answering a type‑3 query: let D = dist(a,b). Let low = (D+1)/2 (integer ceiling). Let high = max(eccentricity(a), eccentricity(b)). Output '1' if low ≤ K ≤ high else '0'. All operations are O(log N), giving total O((N+Q) log N) per test case.

Edge cases: when there is exactly one special node, a = b = that node, D = 0, low = 0, high = eccentricity(a). The formula still works.
solution.timeComplexity: O((N + Q) · log N) per test case (centroid decomposition updates and queries are logarithmic).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N log N) for the centroid decomposition structures and distance arrays.
